And yeah, that ends up being, recurringly, the kind of MRs that introduce new dependencies. Make people feel, feel different feelings, even like, even in comparison to MRs that introduced like 1,000 lines of code have changed. Like a new dependency is like, I don't even know how many lines of runtime code this is changing. Like, like there's so much known that, um, someone who cares about the product can be concerned about. And is the like maturity of the project and the maturity of the release, like, is like, we as GitLab, do we tend to want to use the hottest release stuff or do we wait a bit of time to see if security stuff bubbles up from the releases and from the security perspective, what's the good litmus test for like adding new dependencies?

utterances.speaker: A
utterances.text: Yeah, also hello Anna, someone, someone else joined us. Uh, yeah, we, we definitely want to use the latest version. Um, there is of course a stability and even a security risk, I guess, to being super cutting edge, but chances are that they're fixing security issues. And unfortunately, very few people are as open and transparent as we are with security issues, and they will silently patch everything. So that is a— because a new version does not mention a security fix does not mean there is no security fix.

utterances.speaker: B
utterances.text: Oh wow.

utterances.speaker: A
utterances.text: Unfortunately. So, so many, many companies don't Well, companies, yes, but even less so, like small, like benevolent dictators for life don't require, don't request CVs. They don't request CVs and especially not for lower severity issues. A lot of companies and groups only request CVs for super critical stuff. But what if that low severity issue is just used in a specific condition at GitLab that makes it super critical? So we want to have the latest versions. We're looking for activity in the dependency. We do have one dependency. It's one of our wiki rendering ones. It hasn't been updated in like 11 years, I think. I don't like that. I don't like that at all. And so we have some stuff like that. So you want some sort of activity. And once again, like those are open source projects and one day that person might decide I'm not maintaining this anymore. So it's ideally this would be a kind of ongoing process where like we have some sort of monitoring over that. And but right now the best we can do as a code reviewer is to look, is this being maintained now? So like any commits, any like if it's a gem, you go on RubyGems, like how, what does the release cycle look like?

utterances.speaker: B
utterances.text: Oh yeah, that's interesting.

utterances.speaker: A
utterances.text: And wow, I prefer from the security side, a gem that has like one purpose and does almost only that. It does other stuff, but it's like, it's pretty just a tool for that job. Sometimes you have gems that just do so many things, and it allows you to pivot in. Like, a hacker that finds a, a certain vulnerability could use a feature of that gem that we don't use, but it's there, and it's used to increase the severity of the vulnerability. It happened with another server-side rendering thing. I think it was for the wikis again, but some sort of CSS server-side parsing ended up an arbitrary file read. Like, why did this read files? So, so, so like the person is like, yeah, I'm just doing this CSS stylesheet, and here's your secrets, the.rb file.

We are a CNA because we can, like, open source projects hosted on GitLab can request CVE numbers and we kind of create that for them. You cannot request a CVE for a project owned by a CNA. He had thousands of those. And then he modified a fuzzer like AFL to run on, like, DOM Purify and libraries like that, but not just JavaScript, a bunch of languages. There was one for Java, and I believe he did it for HTML Server Sanitizer in Ruby as well. And he just, I think he had a computer running those things, and he just let it go for a week and a half or something, and then he gets a notification, and then, oh, this thing is not sanitized and he has like the most massive HTML payload and then he works it and figures out what's the minimal thing and the sanitizer reads, parses the HTML and thinks, I don't know, this is a list with an embedded comment or whatever, but the browser parses that and actually does not parse in the same order and sees a script tag instead. And So that was supposed to be text, but it is actually parsed as a tag. So, so that sort of stuff can happen. And also when there's a bug in the HTML sanitizer, we have to update a library and ship a new release.
